questions.text: Which set of ABG values is directly measured from an arterial puncture sample according to the lesson definition?

answers.text: SpO2, heart rate, and blood pressure

questions.text: Which ABG value is used to evaluate the adequacy of ventilation?
Which ABG value is used to evaluate oxygenation?
Which finding is listed as a contraindication to arterial blood gas puncture?

answers.text: Positive ulnar circulation within 5 to 10 seconds

questions.text: For routine adult arterial blood gas sampling, which artery is identified as the vessel of choice?
During a modified Allen test, what should happen after the ulnar artery is released if collateral circulation is adequate?

answers.text: The hand should flush within 5 to 10 seconds
The radial pulse should disappear for 30 seconds
The patient should fully extend the wrist

questions.text: What angle is recommended for inserting the needle during a radial artery blood gas puncture?

answers.text: Nearly perpendicular to the skin surface
30 to 45 degrees or less, bevel up

questions.text: Why should air bubbles be removed from a plastic ABG syringe before analysis?

answers.text: Air bubbles make the pH electrode read only venous values
Room air diffusion can skew the PaO2
Air bubbles automatically clot the sample
Room air eliminates the need for heparin

questions.text: Which anticoagulant is identified as the anticoagulant of choice for arterial blood gas sampling?
Which indication supports placement or use of an indwelling arterial line?

answers.text: Frequent ABG measurements and continuous blood pressure monitoring

questions.text: If an arterial line transducer is positioned below the level of the heart, what happens to the pressure reading?

answers.text: It becomes a capillary blood gas

questions.text: Capillary blood gases are mainly used as an alternative to direct arterial access in which patients?

answers.text: Patients with accurate oxygenation analysis needs

questions.text: Which capillary blood gas value is described as being of little value for estimating arterial oxygenation?
What is the normal arterial blood pH range given in the lesson?
What is the relationship between pH and free hydrogen ion concentration?

answers.text: They are both measured only by pulse oximetry

questions.text: Which systems work together to maintain blood pH homeostasis?

answers.text: Chest wall, ribs, and diaphragm only

questions.text: Most clinical changes in PaCO2 are caused by changes in what?
What happens to PaCO2 when alveolar ventilation increases?

answers.text: PaCO2 becomes the same as PaO2

questions.text: Which form accounts for the major mechanism of CO2 transport from tissues to the lungs in the lesson?
Which macronutrient metabolism ratio produces the most CO2 per mole of O2 consumed?
An RQ greater than 1.0 may suggest which nutritional problem from the lesson?
A low pH shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve in which direction and with what effect?

answers.text: Right, decreasing hemoglobin affinity for O2
Left, decreasing hemoglobin affinity for O2
Right, increasing hemoglobin affinity for O2

questions.text: According to the ROME method, PaCO2 has what relationship with pH?

answers.text: Only a relationship during capillary sampling

questions.text: An ABG has pH 7.30, PaCO2 55 mmHg, and HCO3 24 mEq/L. Using ROME and the lesson's normal ranges, what is the best interpretation?
An ABG has pH 7.50, PaCO2 40 mmHg, and HCO3 30 mEq/L. Using ROME and the lesson's normal ranges, what is the best interpretation?
For a patient younger than 60 on room air at sea level, which PaO2 range is classified as mild hypoxemia?
For patients older than 60, what rule of thumb is given for expected PaO2?

answers.text: Use 45 mmHg as normal for all ages

questions.text: Which value is described as the best index of oxygen transport to the tissues?
Which formula for arterial oxygen content matches the lesson?

answers.text: CaO2 = pH x HCO3 x PaCO2

questions.text: On 100% oxygen, which A-a gradient range is listed as normal?
On 100% oxygen, an A-a gradient greater than 300 torr indicates which problem in the lesson?
Which blood gas electrode is paired with pH analysis in the lesson?
Which blood gas electrode is paired with PCO2 analysis?
Which blood gas electrode is paired with PO2 analysis?
Co-oximetry uses eight light wavelengths to differentiate which group?

answers.text: Hemoglobin species such as Hb, HbO2, MetHb, HbCO, and SHb

questions.text: Which benefit of point-of-care testing is listed in the lesson?

answers.text: Requires larger samples than central lab testing
Shortened turnaround time and more timely intervention
Prevents the need to mix the sample

questions.text: How often does a one-point calibration automatically run according to the lesson?
In quality control, accuracy means what?

answers.text: How closely measured results reflect the actual value
The number of ribs visible on X-ray
The time before a sample clots

questions.text: Which chest X-ray projection is described as the standard position?
Which chest X-ray projection is commonly used for bedridden ICU patients who are too sick to transport?

answers.text: PA projection with the patient standing
AP projection with a portable X-ray machine
Lateral decubitus on the affected side

questions.text: A lateral neck image is valuable for identifying which pediatric problem?

answers.text: Upper airway obstruction such as croup or epiglottitis

questions.text: Which three factors are emphasized when evaluating chest X-ray quality?
Adequate chest X-ray penetration means which structure should be just visible behind the heart?
Adequate inspiration on chest X-ray should show about how many anterior and posterior ribs?

answers.text: 1 to 2 anterior ribs and 3 to 4 posterior ribs
10 to 12 anterior ribs and no posterior ribs
5 to 6 anterior ribs and 8 to 10 posterior ribs

questions.text: Which chest X-ray terminology is associated with COPD in the lesson?

answers.text: Kerley B lines and batwing infiltrates only

questions.text: A chest X-ray finding of consolidation with air bronchograms is associated with which condition?
Which finding is associated with pneumothorax?

answers.text: Fluid in the most dependent pleural region
Rule of two's on a control chart
Visceral pleural line with absent vascular markings and increased lucency

questions.text: Left heart failure is associated with which chest X-ray terminology?

answers.text: Increased vascular markings, batwing infiltrates, and Kerley B lines
Absent vascular markings in one hemithorax only

questions.text: Which terminology is associated with ARDS in the lesson?

answers.text: Bilateral diffuse infiltrates, poorly defined opacities, and fluffy or patchy ground glass appearance
Hyperinflated lungs with increased retrosternal airspace

questions.text: In a V/Q scan, what does a normal ventilation scan with an abnormal perfusion scan indicate?
